docs.title: Learn Embedded Basics
docs.text:
Baremetal firmware from first principles — registers, peripherals, drivers, and MCU bring-up.

  • Getting Started

    Learning path · Toolchain setup · Datasheet reading

    Begin here

  • Fundamentals

    Memory-mapped IO · Startup code · Linker scripts · Debugging

    Core concepts

  • Peripherals

    GPIO · UART · SPI · I2C · Timers · PWM · ADC · Interrupts

    Peripheral guide

  • Drivers

    HAL-style driver architecture for GPIO, UART, I2C, SPI

    Driver patterns

  • MCU Profiles

    STM32F103 · AVR ATmega328P · RP2040 · MSP430 · nRF52832

    MCU reference

docs.title: Embedded Dev Stack
docs.text:
graph TD
    App["Application Layer
(your firmware logic)"]
    DRV["Driver Layer
(GPIO, UART, SPI, I2C, Timers)"]
    HAL["Hardware Abstraction
(register-level access)"]
    HW["Hardware
(MCU registers, pins, peripherals)"]
    App --> DRV --> HAL --> HW

docs.title: Learning Phases
docs.text: Phase Focus Milestone 1 C for embedded, number systems, memory GPIO register write toggles LED 2 Startup code, clocks, MMIO UART banner from cold reset 3 Timers, PWM, UART, SPI, I2C, ADC Multi-peripheral app with stable timing 4 Tooling: GDB, OpenOCD, logic analyser Debug firmware without printf

docs.title: Register Bit Operations
docs.text:
/* Set bit N */      REG |=  (1u << N);
/* Clear bit N */    REG &= ~(1u << N);
/* Toggle bit N */   REG ^=  (1u << N);
/* Read bit N */     bool b = (REG >> N) & 1u;
/* Write field */    REG = (REG & ~MASK) | (value << SHIFT);
/* Atomic set */     GPIO->BSRR = (1u << N);        // set
/* Atomic clear */   GPIO->BSRR = (1u << (N + 16)); // reset

docs.title: Typical ARM Cortex-M Memory Map
docs.text: Address Range Region 0x00000000–0x1FFFFFFF Code (flash, ROM) 0x20000000–0x3FFFFFFF SRAM 0x40000000–0x5FFFFFFF Peripheral registers (APB, AHB) 0xE0000000+ System (NVIC, SysTick, DWT)

docs.title: Peripheral Init Checklist
docs.text:
Universal Peripheral Init Order

  1. Enable peripheral clock (RCC/CMU/CLK register)
  2. Configure GPIO pins (mode, AF, pull, speed)
  3. Configure peripheral registers (baud, mode, etc.)
  4. Enable interrupts if needed (peripheral IE + NVIC)
  5. Start the peripheral (UE/EN bit)

docs.title: UART Baud Rate Formula
docs.text:
BRR = PCLK_Hz / baud_rate

Example: 72MHz / 115200 = 625

docs.title: PWM Period & Duty
docs.text:
Timer frequency = PCLK / (PSC + 1)
PWM period      = Timer freq / (ARR + 1)
Duty cycle      = CCR / (ARR + 1) × 100%

docs.title: Common Embedded Bugs
docs.text: Symptom Likely Cause Hard fault on reset Missing peripheral clock, wrong stack pointer GPIO doesn't toggle Peripheral clock not enabled UART garbage output Wrong baud rate, missing pin AF config I2C hangs Missing pull-up resistors, wrong device address ADC reads 0 or 4095 always Wrong reference, analog pin configured as digital Timer ISR fires too fast/slow PSC/ARR formula error Global variable has wrong value .data not copied from flash (startup bug)

docs.title: GPIO Mode Bits (STM32F4+)
docs.text: MODER Mode OTYPER Type 00 Input 0 Push-pull 01 Output 1 Open-drain 10 Alternate function 11 Analog

docs.title: Pattern 1 — Bit Field Access
docs.text:
// Safe read-modify-write
#define BIT(n)         (1u << (n))
#define SET_BIT(r,n)   ((r) |=  BIT(n))
#define CLR_BIT(r,n)   ((r) &= ~BIT(n))
#define TGL_BIT(r,n)   ((r) ^=  BIT(n))
#define READ_BIT(r,n)  (((r) >> (n)) & 1u)

docs.title: Pattern 2 — Non-Blocking Delay (Tick Timer)
docs.text:
static volatile uint32_t ticks = 0;

void SysTick_Handler(void) { ticks++; }

uint32_t get_ticks(void)    { return ticks; }

bool timeout(uint32_t start, uint32_t ms) {
    return (get_ticks() - start) >= ms;
}

// Usage
uint32_t t = get_ticks();
do_something();
while (!timeout(t, 100));  // wait up to 100ms

docs.title: Pattern 3 — State Machine ISR
docs.text:
typedef enum { STATE_IDLE, STATE_BUSY, STATE_DONE, STATE_ERR } state_t;
static volatile state_t state = STATE_IDLE;

void DMA_IRQHandler(void) {
    if (DMA->ISR & DMA_ISR_TCIF) {
        DMA->IFCR = DMA_IFCR_CTCIF;   // clear flag
        state = STATE_DONE;
    } else if (DMA->ISR & DMA_ISR_TEIF) {
        state = STATE_ERR;
    }
}

// Application
state = STATE_BUSY;
dma_start_transfer();
while (state == STATE_BUSY);  // or use RTOS semaphore
if (state == STATE_ERR) handle_error();

docs.title: Pattern 4 — Ring Buffer
docs.text:
typedef struct {
    uint8_t  data[256];   // power of 2 for fast modulo
    uint8_t  head, tail;  // 8-bit wraps at 256 automatically
} rb_t;

static inline bool rb_push(rb_t *b, uint8_t v) {
    if ((uint8_t)(b->head - b->tail) == 256u - 1u) return false;
    b->data[b->head++] = v;
    return true;
}
static inline bool rb_pop(rb_t *b, uint8_t *v) {
    if (b->head == b->tail) return false;
    *v = b->data[b->tail++];
    return true;
}

docs.title: Pattern 5 — Error Code Propagation
docs.text:
drv_status_t init_system(void) {
    drv_status_t s;
    if ((s = gpio_driver_init()) != DRV_OK) return s;
    if ((s = uart_driver_init(115200)) != DRV_OK) return s;
    if ((s = i2c_driver_init(I2C_400KHZ)) != DRV_OK) return s;
    return DRV_OK;
}

Pattern Recognition for Embedded

  • Need precise timing? → Timer + interrupt
  • Need fast data transfer? → DMA (not polling)
  • Need event-driven response? → EXTI interrupt
  • Need non-blocking operation? → State machine + ISR flag
  • Need portable code? → HAL driver layer
  • Need ultra-low power? → Sleep modes + WFI/WFE

docs.title: Driver Architecture
docs.text:
• GPIO Driver — Portable GPIO HAL
  • UART Driver — Ring-buffer async UART
  • I2C Driver — Master read/write API
  • SPI Driver — Full-duplex SPI master

Driver Design Principles

  • Portable: abstract MCU-specific registers behind a clean API
  • Non-blocking: use callbacks/flags, not polling loops
  • Minimal coupling: driver doesn't know about the application

docs.title: GPIO Driver
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Understand the HAL-style GPIO driver API
  • Use gpio_pin_t struct for portable pin references
  • Attach interrupt callbacks via the driver

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: A well-designed GPIO driver abstracts the MCU's register layout behind a clean API. The same application code works across STM32, AVR, and RP2040 by swapping the driver implementation.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
classDiagram
    class gpio_pin_t {
        uint8_t port
        uint8_t pin
    }
    class gpio_driver {
        +gpio_driver_init()
        +gpio_init(pin, mode, pull)
        +gpio_write(pin, state)
        +gpio_read(pin, out_state)
        +gpio_toggle(pin)
        +gpio_attach_irq(pin, edge, cb, ctx)
    }
    gpio_driver --> gpio_pin_t

docs.title: Code Examples
Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • Always validate pin parameters in the driver (null check, port/pin range check) — return DRV_ERR_INVALID instead of hard-faulting
  • The callback is called from ISR context — keep it minimal

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
What is drv_status_t used for?

A return code enum (DRV_OK, DRV_ERR_BUSY, DRV_ERR_INVALID, DRV_ERR_TIMEOUT) that lets callers handle errors without exceptions.

How does gpio_attach_irq differ from directly writing EXTI registers?

The driver abstracts EXTI/port selection, NVIC setup, and callback registration. Application code expresses intent (edge, callback) not mechanism (AFIO, EXTICR bits).

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: GPIO driver: portable API with gpio_pin_t struct. Driver handles register-level details; app uses init/write/read/toggle/attach_irq. Return drv_status_t for all operations.

docs.title: I2C Driver
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Implement I2C master write and read transactions
  • Handle timeout and bus error recovery
  • Support common sensor register access pattern

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: I2C driver exposes i2c_write_reg and i2c_read_reg that hide the START/address/ACK/STOP state machine from the application.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> START: transaction request
    START --> ADDR_W: START sent
    ADDR_W --> REG: ACK received
    REG --> RSTART: register sent (for read)
    REG --> DATA_W: data phase (for write)
    RSTART --> ADDR_R: repeated START
    ADDR_R --> DATA_R: ACK received
    DATA_R --> STOP: all bytes read
    STOP --> IDLE

docs.title: Code Examples
Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • Add timeout to every polling loop (waiting for SR1 flags). Without timeout, a disconnected device causes infinite loop
  • After bus error, send 9 SCL clocks and STOP to reset the bus

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
How to detect I2C address of an unknown device?

Scan: for addr in 0x08..0x77: send START + addr, if ACK received the device exists at that address.

What causes 'bus busy' even after reset?

Slave held SDA low during a transaction that was interrupted. Recovery: generate 9 SCL pulses then a STOP condition.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: I2C driver: abstract START/ADDR/ACK/STOP behind read_reg/write_reg API. Add timeout to all polling waits. Handle bus errors with recovery sequence.

docs.title: SPI Driver
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Implement SPI master with configurable mode and baud rate
  • Support DMA-based transfers for large buffers
  • Manage multiple devices on the same SPI bus

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: SPI driver exposes spi_transfer (full-duplex) and spi_read/write wrappers. CS management is typically left to the device driver layer above.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    App["Application
(sensor/flash driver)"] --> CS["CS assert
(device-specific)"]
    CS --> DRV["spi_transfer(tx)"]
    DRV --> HW["SPI Hardware
(TXE/RXNE)"]
    HW --> DRV
    DRV --> CS2["CS deassert"]

docs.title: Code Examples
Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • Always wait for BSY flag to clear before deasserting CS (some MCUs latch the last byte after TXE goes high but before transmit completes)
  • Different devices on the same bus may need different CPOL/CPHA — reconfigure SPI or use dedicated SPI buses

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
SPI DMA benefit?

CPU is free during large transfers. For a 256-byte flash read at 10MHz SPI, polling takes ~200µs of CPU time. DMA takes ~0µs (CPU continues other work).

How to share one SPI bus between two devices with different modes?

Disable SPI (CR1 SPE=0), change CPOL/CPHA, re-enable. Or use hardware NSS with proper CS timing.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: SPI driver: wait TXE, write DR, wait RXNE, read DR. Wait BSY before CS deassert. DMA for large transfers. CS management in device-layer above driver.

docs.title: UART Driver
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Design a non-blocking UART driver with interrupt RX
  • Expose simple read/write byte API
  • Use ring buffers for TX and RX queues

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: A production UART driver never blocks. It queues bytes for TX (interrupt-driven drain) and stores received bytes in a ring buffer for the application to consume at its own pace.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    App["Application"] -->|uart_write| TxBuf["TX Ring Buffer"]
    TxBuf -->|TXE interrupt drains| UART_HW["UART Hardware"]
    UART_HW -->|RXNE interrupt fills| RxBuf["RX Ring Buffer"]
    RxBuf -->|uart_read| App

docs.title: Code Examples
Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • Buffer size must be a power of 2 if you optimize with & (SIZE-1) instead of % SIZE
  • RX buffer overrun (buffer full, ISR drops byte) is silent — consider an overflow counter for diagnostics

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
Why have both TX and RX ring buffers?

TX: application submits bytes, ISR drains them when UART is ready (non-blocking write). RX: ISR deposits bytes immediately, application reads at its own pace.

How to implement uart_write_string efficiently?

Copy string to TX ring buffer, then enable TXE interrupt. ISR pops one byte per TXE event, disables TXE interrupt when buffer empties.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: UART driver: TX ring buffer (ISR drains on TXE), RX ring buffer (ISR fills on RXNE). App reads/writes non-blocking. Never block in ISR.

docs.title: Fundamentals
docs.text:
• Memory-Mapped IO — Register access patterns
  • Startup Code — From reset to main()
  • Linker Scripts — Memory layout control
  • Debugging — GDB, OpenOCD, JTAG/SWD

docs.title: Embedded Debugging
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Use GDB + OpenOCD for source-level debugging
  • Set breakpoints, inspect registers, and step through code
  • Use JTAG/SWD for flashing and real-time debug

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text:
Embedded Debugging Mental Model

You have three windows into the hardware: GDB (software state), logic analyser (bus traffic), and oscilloscope (analog signals). Learn to use all three.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    GDB["GDB Client
(source-level debug)"] <-->|"RSP protocol
tcp:3333"| OpenOCD["OpenOCD
(debug server)"]
    OpenOCD <-->|"JTAG/SWD"| Probe["Debug Probe
(ST-Link, J-Link)"]
    Probe <-->|"SWD pins
SWDIO, SWDCLK"| MCU["Target MCU"]

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
GDB Quick ReferenceHard Fault Debugging 
# Connect GDB to OpenOCD
arm-none-eabi-gdb firmware.elf
(gdb) target remote :3333
(gdb) monitor reset halt   # reset and halt at reset vector

# Breakpoints
(gdb) break main           # break at function
(gdb) break gpio.c:42      # break at file:line
(gdb) watch *0x40010C0C    # break on register write

# Stepping
(gdb) continue    # run until breakpoint
(gdb) next        # step over (source line)
(gdb) step        # step into
(gdb) finish      # run until function returns

# Inspect
(gdb) print variable
(gdb) x/4wx 0x40010800    # read 4 words from address
(gdb) info registers       # all CPU registers

```c // Hard fault handler to capture CPU state void HardFault_Handler(void) { __asm volatile ( "tst lr, #4

" "ite eq " "mrseq r0, msp " "mrsne r0, psp " "b HardFault_HandlerC " ); } void HardFault_HandlerC(uint32_t *sp) { // sp[0]=r0, sp[1]=r1, sp[2]=r2, sp[3]=r3 // sp[4]=r12, sp[5]=lr, sp[6]=pc, sp[7]=xpsr uint32_t pc = sp[6]; // fault address while (1); // breakpoint here, inspect pc } ```

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • OpenOCD must be running before GDB connects
  • Hard faults with garbage PC usually mean stack overflow or corrupted vector table

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
What does SWD stand for and what pins does it use?

Serial Wire Debug — uses 2 pins: SWDIO (data) and SWDCLK (clock). Lower pin count than JTAG's 4+ pins.

How to find a hard fault cause without a debugger?

Toggle a GPIO before each major code section. The last toggled LED tells you where the fault occurred.

What is ITM (Instrumentation Trace Macrocell)?

A hardware debug channel on Cortex-M3/M4. Allows printf-style output via SWO pin without UART. Zero overhead compared to UART debug.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Debugging: GDB + OpenOCD + debug probe. Key commands: break, watch, print, x. Hard fault: capture stacked PC. SWD = 2-pin debug interface.

docs.title: Linker Scripts
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Read and write a minimal ARM linker script
  • Define FLASH and RAM memory regions
  • Place sections (.text, .data, .bss) correctly

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: The linker script is the map of your firmware's memory. It tells the linker where to place code and data, and provides symbols the startup code uses to copy/zero sections.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[".text
(code, const)"] --> FLASH["FLASH
0x08000000
64KB"]
    B[".data init values"] --> FLASH
    C[".data
(rw initialized)"] --> RAM["RAM
0x20000000
20KB"]
    D[".bss
(rw zero-init)"] --> RAM
    E["Stack
(grows down)"] --> RAM
    F["Heap (optional)"] --> RAM

docs.title: Code Examples
Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • Vector table section must come first — it must be at the start of flash
  • AT > FLASH places .data's load address in flash while its runtime address is in RAM

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
What is the difference between LMA and VMA?

LMA (Load Memory Address) = where the data lives in flash. VMA (Virtual Memory Address) = where the CPU accesses it at runtime. For .data, LMA is in flash, VMA is in RAM.

Why is the vector table placed first?

ARM Cortex-M boots from address 0 (or remapped flash start). Entry [0] must be the initial stack pointer, [1] must be the reset handler address.

How do you add a heap?

After .bss, add: _heap_start = .; . += HEAP_SIZE; _heap_end = .; and implement sbrk() using these symbols.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Linker script: MEMORY (regions) + SECTIONS (placement). .text/.rodata in FLASH. .data: LMA=FLASH, VMA=RAM. .bss zeroed in RAM. _estack = top of RAM.

docs.title: Memory-Mapped IO
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Understand how peripheral registers are mapped to memory addresses
  • Write correct register access code with volatile
  • Identify and avoid common MMIO bugs

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text:
The CPU has one address space. Peripherals live in it — there's no separate I/O bus on ARM Cortex-M. Reading/writing an address can toggle a pin, configure a timer, or read ADC data.

Memory Anchor

MMIO = treat hardware like memory. But hardware has side effects — reads can clear flags, writes trigger actions. volatile tells the compiler not to optimize these away.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
graph LR
    CPU["CPU"] --> |"load/store
(normal instructions)"| AddressSpace["Address Space"]
    AddressSpace --> RAM["RAM
0x20000000+"]
    AddressSpace --> Flash["Flash
0x08000000+"]
    AddressSpace --> APB1["APB1 Peripherals
(UART, I2C, SPI, Timers)"]
    AddressSpace --> APB2["APB2 Peripherals
(GPIO, ADC, USART1)"]
    AddressSpace --> AHB["AHB (DMA, RCC, CRC)"]

docs.title: Code Examples
Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • Missing volatile allows compiler to cache register reads in a CPU register — changes to hardware state become invisible
  • Never use int* for peripheral registers — always volatile uint32_t*

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
Why is volatile required for peripheral registers?

Without volatile, the compiler may optimize repeated reads into one, or skip writes it thinks are redundant. Peripheral registers have hardware side effects that make every access meaningful.

What is the difference between reading UART data register and a RAM variable?

Reading UART DR clears the RXNE (receive not empty) flag — it has a hardware side effect. RAM reads are pure.

What does 'reserved bits must be kept at reset value' mean?

These bits have undocumented internal functions. Modifying them can corrupt the peripheral's state machine. Always read-modify-write.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: MMIO: peripherals = memory addresses. volatile prevents optimization. Bit ops: |= set, &=~ clear, ^= toggle. Always read-modify-write to preserve other bits.

docs.title: Startup Code
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Explain what happens between reset and main()
  • Implement a minimal reset handler in C
  • Understand .data, .bss, and linker symbols

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text:
When the MCU resets, the CPU doesn't magically start at main(). It starts at the reset vector — a function that must prepare memory before your code can run.

Memory Anchor: CDP → main

Copy .data from flash → Delete (zero) .bss → Preamble (SystemInit) → main

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant HW as Hardware Reset
    participant VT as Vector Table
    participant SH as Reset Handler
    participant CRT as C Runtime
    participant main as main()

    HW->>VT: Fetch reset vector address
    VT->>SH: Jump to Reset_Handler
    SH->>CRT: Copy .data from flash to RAM
    SH->>CRT: Zero .bss segment
    SH->>CRT: Call SystemInit (optional)
    SH->>main: Branch to main()

docs.title: Code Examples
Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • If .data copy is wrong, global variables have garbage values at startup
  • If .bss is not zeroed, uninitialised globals have random values (C standard requires zero-init)

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
Where is .data at reset?

In flash (non-volatile). The startup code copies it to RAM where the CPU can write it.

What is .bss?

The uninitialized data section. C standard guarantees global/static variables with no explicit initializer are zero. Startup code zeroes this region.

How does the CPU find Reset_Handler?

The vector table is at address 0x00000000 (or 0x08000000 for STM32 flash boot). Entry [1] is the reset vector address. CPU fetches it on reset.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Startup: copy .data from flash to RAM, zero .bss, call SystemInit, call main. Vector table at address 0 provides the reset handler address.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
• Learning Path — Your 4-phase roadmap
  • Toolchain Setup — GCC, OpenOCD, CMake
  • Datasheet Reading — Decode register maps

docs.title: Datasheet Reading
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Navigate a MCU reference manual efficiently
  • Decode register bit fields
  • Identify the initialization sequence for any peripheral

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text:
The Most Important Skill

Every embedded bug ultimately traces back to a register. Learning to read datasheets converts mysteries into 10-minute fixes.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[Need to use UART] --> B[Find UART chapter in RM]
    B --> C[Read functional description]
    C --> D[Find register map]
    D --> E[Identify init sequence]
    E --> F[Check bit fields and reset values]
    F --> G[Write and test code]

docs.title: Code Examples
Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • Always check reset values before assuming registers are 0
  • W1C (write-1-to-clear) bits: writing 0 has no effect, writing 1 clears the flag

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
What does 'Reserved bits must be kept at reset value' mean?

Some bits are undocumented but used internally. Writing wrong values corrupts the peripheral's state machine. Always read-modify-write.

Where do you find the peripheral clock enable bit?

In the RCC (Reset and Clock Control) chapter. Each peripheral has an enable bit in APBxENR or AHBxENR.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Datasheet reading: functional description → register map → init sequence → bit fields. Always check: clock enable, pin mux, register reset values, access type (RO/RW/W1C).

docs.title: Learning Path
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Understand the 4-phase embedded learning roadmap
  • Know what to practice at each phase
  • Identify your current level

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text:
Start Here

Embedded development rewards depth over breadth. Master one peripheral at a time, bring up real hardware at each step.

This path moves from fundamental C and hardware concepts through driver architecture. Each phase has a concrete milestone that proves you've internalized the concepts.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
graph LR
    P1["Phase 1
Core Foundations
Weeks 1-2"] --> P2["Phase 2
Baremetal Bring-Up
Weeks 3-4"]
    P2 --> P3["Phase 3
Peripheral Mastery
Weeks 5-7"]
    P3 --> P4["Phase 4
Tooling & Debugging
Weeks 8-9"]

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Phase 1 — Core FoundationsPhase 2 — Baremetal Bring-UpPhase 3 — Peripheral MasteryPhase 4 — Tooling & Debugging

Focus: - C pointers, bitwise ops, structs for embedded - Number systems: hex/binary, endianness, memory layout - Digital electronics basics: voltage, pull-up/down, debounce

Milestone: Explain how a GPIO register write toggles an LED.

Focus: - Startup code and vector tables - Clock initialization and reset flow - Memory-mapped I/O and register access patterns

Practice: LED blink and UART banner from cold reset (no HAL).

Focus: - Timers, PWM, UART, SPI, I2C, ADC, Interrupts - Polling vs interrupt-driven design - Error handling and timeout patterns

Milestone: Read sensor over I2C and print via UART.

Focus: - GDB + OpenOCD for source-level debugging - Logic analyser for bus protocol verification - JTAG/SWD flashing and boundary scan - Linker scripts and memory map inspection

Milestone: Debug a firmware bug without printf.

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • Don't skip Phase 1 — missing bit manipulation knowledge causes subtle bugs later
  • Don't use vendor HAL until you understand the registers it wraps

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
What is the first peripheral to bring up on a new MCU?

GPIO output — blink an LED. It verifies: clock enabled, pin configured, write works, your toolchain produces correct binaries.

Why avoid printf for early debugging?

UART requires clock setup, pin mux, and baud configuration. If any of those fail, printf silently drops output. Use GPIO toggle or SWD breakpoints first.

What is the key skill gap between beginner and competent embedded developers?

Understanding the hardware state machine — knowing which registers to configure, in what order, and what side effects each write has.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: 4 phases: Foundations → Bring-Up → Peripherals → Tooling. Each phase has a concrete hardware milestone. Master GPIO before UART; UART before SPI/I2C.

docs.title: Toolchain Setup
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Install arm-none-eabi-gcc cross-compiler
  • Set up OpenOCD for flashing and debugging
  • Use CMake for portable build configuration

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: The toolchain is your forge. Getting it right once saves hours of debugging mysterious build errors.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    A[Source .c/.h] --> B["arm-none-eabi-gcc
(compile)"]
    B --> C[".o object files"]
    C --> D["arm-none-eabi-ld
(link with .ld script)"]
    D --> E[".elf binary"]
    E --> F["objcopy → .bin/.hex"]
    F --> G["OpenOCD/ST-Link
(flash to MCU)"]

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
ARM GCC InstallMinimal MakefileOpenOCD Flash 
# Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt install gcc-arm-none-eabi binutils-arm-none-eabi

# Verify
arm-none-eabi-gcc --version

CC      = arm-none-eabi-gcc
CFLAGS  = -mcpu=cortex-m3 -mthumb -O2 -Wall
LDFLAGS = -T linker.ld -nostartfiles

all: firmware.elf

firmware.elf: main.o startup.o
    $(CC) $(LDFLAGS) -o $@ $^

%.o: %.c
    $(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

# Flash STM32 via ST-Link
openocd -f interface/stlink.cfg -f target/stm32f1x.cfg \
  -c "program firmware.elf verify reset exit"

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • Use the right -mcpu flag for your MCU (cortex-m0, m3, m4, m4f). Wrong flag generates incompatible instructions
  • Always include -nostartfiles when supplying your own startup code

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
What flags are mandatory for Cortex-M?

-mcpu=cortex-mX -mthumb — Thumb instruction set is required. For M4F with FPU add -mfpu=fpv4-sp-d16 -mfloat-abi=hard.

What does objcopy do?

Converts .elf (with debug info) to .bin or .hex (raw binary for flashing). Use arm-none-eabi-objcopy -O binary firmware.elf firmware.bin.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Toolchain: arm-none-eabi-gcc + OpenOCD. Build pipeline: .c → .o → .elf → .bin → flash. CMake wraps this portably.

docs.title: MCU Profiles
docs.text:
• STM32F103 — ARM Cortex-M3, 72MHz
  • AVR ATmega328P — 8-bit, Arduino compatible
  • RP2040 — Dual Cortex-M0+, PIO
  • MSP430 — Ultra-low power
  • nRF52832 — Cortex-M4F + BLE

docs.title: Bring-Up Checklist
docs.text:
Verified Working

  • [ ] LED output toggles at expected rate
  • [ ] UART banner at 115200 baud on cold reset
  • [ ] Button input with pull-up reads correctly
  • [ ] Timer 1ms tick fires reliably
  • [ ] GDB breakpoint hits in main()

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: See the peripheral guides for register-level details. This MCU's specifics are in the reference manual; this page captures the bring-up essentials.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: See the peripheral guides for register-level details. This MCU's specifics are in the reference manual; this page captures the bring-up essentials.

docs.title: Peripherals
docs.text:
• GPIO — Digital I/O, interrupts
  • UART — Async serial communication
  • SPI — Synchronous serial (full-duplex)
  • I2C — Two-wire bus, multi-device
  • Timers — PWM, capture, compare
  • PWM — Pulse width modulation
  • ADC — Analog-to-digital conversion
  • Interrupts — Event-driven programming

docs.title: ADC — Analog to Digital Conversion
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Configure ADC for single conversion mode
  • Read ADC result and convert to voltage
  • Understand resolution, reference voltage, and sampling time

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: ADC converts an analog voltage (0 to VREF) into a digital number (0 to 2^N - 1). The MCU can then process sensor data from temperature sensors, potentiometers, etc.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
graph LR
    A["Analog Input
0..VREF volts"] --> B["Sample & Hold"]
    B --> C["Successive Approximation
N-bit SAR ADC"]
    C --> D["Digital Output
0..2^N-1"]
    D --> E["Voltage = result * VREF / 2^N"]

docs.title: Code Examples
Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • ADC clock must be ≤ 14MHz (STM32F1). Set prescaler accordingly
  • Longer sampling time improves accuracy for high-impedance sources

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
12-bit ADC reading of 2048 with 3.3V reference = ?

2048 * 3.3 / 4096 = 1.65V (exactly half of reference).

What is oversampling and why use it?

Taking multiple ADC readings and averaging. Each doubling of samples adds 0.5 bits of effective resolution. Useful when you need > 12 bits without external ADC.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: ADC: N-bit result = analog_voltage * 2^N / VREF. 12-bit STM32: 0-4095 maps to 0-3.3V. Longer sampling = better accuracy for high-impedance.

docs.title: GPIO — General Purpose Input/Output
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Configure GPIO pin as input/output/alternate function
  • Enable peripheral clock before accessing GPIO registers
  • Use pull-up/pull-down resistors correctly
  • Implement GPIO interrupts via EXTI

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text:
GPIO is the first peripheral to master. Every embedded bring-up starts here: blink an LED to verify your toolchain, clock config, and pin access all work.

3-step GPIO recipe

  1. Enable clock for the GPIO port (e.g., RCC→APB2ENR)
  2. Configure mode (input/output/AF/analog) + speed/pull
  3. Access data via IDR (input) or ODR/BSRR (output)

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A["1. Enable GPIO Clock
RCC→APB2ENR |= GPIO_EN"] --> B["2. Configure Pin Mode
GPIO→MODER/CRL/CRH"]
    B --> C{Input or Output?}
    C -->|Output| D["Write ODR or BSRR
(atomic set/reset)"]
    C -->|Input| E["Read IDR
(check bit)"]
    C -->|Interrupt| F["Configure EXTI
NVIC enable"]

docs.title: Code Examples
Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • Peripheral clock must be enabled before accessing any peripheral register — reading/writing without clock causes hard fault
  • Use BSRR (not ODR) for atomic set/reset to avoid race conditions in interrupt-driven code

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
Why use BSRR instead of ODR for output?

BSRR is a write-only atomic set/reset register. Writing bit N sets pin N; writing bit N+16 resets it. No read-modify-write needed — eliminates race conditions in ISRs.

How to configure a pin as open-drain?

Set OTYPER bit for that pin (STM32F4+) or configure CNF bits to 01 (open-drain output) in CRL/CRH (STM32F1). Open-drain is required for I2C lines and wired-OR logic.

What is a floating input and why is it bad?

A floating (unconnected) input has no defined voltage level. It picks up noise and toggles randomly. Always configure unused inputs with pull-up or pull-down.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: GPIO: enable clock → configure mode → read/write. Use BSRR for atomic output. EXTI for interrupts: configure trigger, unmask, enable NVIC, clear pending in ISR.

docs.title: I2C — Two-Wire Bus
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Configure I2C at standard (100kHz) or fast (400kHz) speed
  • Perform read and write transactions with 7-bit addressing
  • Handle ACK/NACK and bus errors

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text:
I2C uses just 2 wires (SDA + SCL) and supports multiple devices on the same bus — each identified by a 7-bit address. It's slower than SPI but simpler wiring for sensors and EEPROMs.

I2C frame structure

START → 7-bit address + R/W bit → ACK → data bytes → ACK → STOP

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Master
    participant S as Slave (addr 0x68)

    M->>S: START
    M->>S: Address 0x68 + W
    S->>M: ACK
    M->>S: Register 0x3B
    S->>M: ACK
    M->>S: REPEATED START
    M->>S: Address 0x68 + R
    S->>M: ACK
    S->>M: Data byte 1
    M->>S: ACK
    S->>M: Data byte 2
    M->>S: NACK
    M->>S: STOP

docs.title: Code Examples
Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • I2C lines must be open-drain with pull-up resistors (typically 4.7kΩ for 100kHz, 2.2kΩ for 400kHz)
  • Bus lockup: if SDA stays low, master must generate 9 clock pulses to free it

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
How to find a device's I2C address?

Bus scan: iterate 0x08 to 0x77, send address byte, check for ACK. Most sensors have fixed addresses in datasheet; some have configurable LSB bits.

What causes I2C bus lockup?

Master reset during a transaction can leave slave clock-stretched. The slave holds SDA low waiting for more clocks. Recovery: send 9 SCLK pulses then STOP.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: I2C: 2 wires, open-drain, 7-bit addresses. Pattern: START → addr+W → ACK → data → STOP. Read: write reg addr, repeated START, addr+R, read bytes, NACK last, STOP.

docs.title: Interrupts
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Understand NVIC priority levels and preemption
  • Write a correct ISR (clear flags, minimal work)
  • Avoid common interrupt bugs: reentrance, race conditions

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text:
Interrupts make embedded systems responsive. Without them, you'd poll every peripheral constantly. With them, the CPU does real work and reacts to events only when they occur.

ISR Golden Rules

  1. Keep ISRs short — set a flag, copy data, return
  2. Clear the interrupt flag at the start of the ISR
  3. Never block or use delay functions inside an ISR

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU (main loop)
    participant NVIC as NVIC
    participant ISR as ISR

    CPU->>CPU: Running main loop
    NVIC->>CPU: Interrupt request (e.g. UART RX)
    CPU->>CPU: Complete current instruction
    CPU->>CPU: Push registers to stack
    CPU->>ISR: Jump to ISR handler
    ISR->>ISR: Clear interrupt flag
    ISR->>ISR: Handle event (copy data, set flag)
    ISR->>CPU: Return from interrupt
    CPU->>CPU: Pop registers, resume main loop

docs.title: Code Examples
Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • Never read hardware registers via volatile variable without disabling interrupts if the ISR also modifies it (race condition)
  • Not clearing the interrupt flag causes the ISR to fire again immediately — infinite interrupt loop

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
What is interrupt latency?

Time from interrupt assertion to first ISR instruction. For Cortex-M it's typically 12-20 clock cycles (register stacking overhead).

What is priority inversion?

A high-priority task waiting for a shared resource held by a low-priority task, while a medium-priority task runs. Solution: priority inheritance mutex.

volatile vs mutex for ISR shared data?

volatile ensures the compiler reads/writes the variable every time. A mutex prevents race conditions between interrupts and main code. Use both for multi-byte data.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: ISR rules: clear flag first, minimal work, no blocking. Share data via volatile flags. Critical sections: disable_irq/enable_irq. NVIC: lower number = higher priority.

docs.title: PWM — Pulse Width Modulation
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Generate PWM signal using timer compare channel
  • Control duty cycle via CCR register
  • Use PWM for motor speed control and LED brightness

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: PWM simulates analog output from a digital pin: rapidly switch between high and low at a fixed frequency; vary the duty cycle to control average power.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
graph LR
    A["Timer CNT counts 0→ARR"] --> B{CNT < CCR?}
    B -->|Yes| C["Output HIGH"]
    B -->|No| D["Output LOW"]
    C --> E["Duty = CCR/ARR × 100%"]
    D --> E

docs.title: Code Examples
Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • PWM frequency = timer frequency / (ARR+1). Duty cycle = CCR / (ARR+1) × 100%
  • Higher ARR gives finer duty cycle resolution but lower PWM frequency

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
What PWM frequency is suitable for motor control?

Typically 10kHz–50kHz. Below audible range (>20kHz) eliminates motor whine. Higher frequency needs faster gate drivers.

What is center-aligned PWM vs edge-aligned?

Edge-aligned: counter counts 0→ARR then resets. Center-aligned: counter counts 0→ARR→0. Center-aligned reduces EMI by spreading switching events.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: PWM: timer compare mode. Duty = CCR/(ARR+1). Set ARR for period, CCR for duty. Higher ARR = finer resolution but lower frequency.

docs.title: SPI — Synchronous Serial (Full-Duplex)
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Configure SPI as master (CPOL, CPHA, baud rate)
  • Perform full-duplex byte transfer
  • Manage chip select (CS) manually for multi-device buses

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text:
SPI uses a shared clock and is full-duplex: master and slave exchange bytes simultaneously. CS (chip select) is active-low and controlled manually.

SPI in one sentence

Shared clock (SCLK) + MOSI (master out) + MISO (master in) + CS per device. 4-wire, full-duplex.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Master
    participant S as Slave

    M->>S: CS low (assert)
    loop 8 clock cycles
        M->>S: MOSI bit
        S->>M: MISO bit
    end
    M->>S: CS high (deassert)

docs.title: Code Examples
Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • CPOL/CPHA must match the slave device's datasheet (4 modes: 0,0 / 0,1 / 1,0 / 1,1)
  • Always deassert CS between transactions — some devices require CS high for a minimum time

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
What are the 4 SPI modes?

Mode 0 (CPOL=0,CPHA=0): idle low, sample rising. Mode 1 (CPOL=0,CPHA=1): idle low, sample falling. Mode 2/3 mirror with idle high. Mode 0 is most common.

Why is SPI faster than I2C?

SPI is full-duplex with no acknowledgement overhead. I2C has ACK bits after every byte and open-drain bus limits speed.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: SPI: 4 wires, full-duplex. Configure CPOL/CPHA to match slave. CS manually controlled. Transfer: wait TXE → write DR → wait RXNE → read DR.

docs.title: Timers
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Configure a timer for periodic interrupts (system tick)
  • Understand prescaler, auto-reload register, and counter
  • Use input capture to measure pulse width

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Timers are the heartbeat of embedded systems — periodic interrupts, PWM generation, input capture, and event counting all use timers.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    CLK["APB Clock
72MHz"] --> PSC["Prescaler
(PSC register)"]
    PSC --> CNT["Counter
(counts 0..ARR)"]
    CNT -->|"CNT == ARR"| OVF["Overflow Event
(update interrupt)"]
    OVF --> CNT
    CNT --> CCR["Compare Channel
(PWM output when CNT==CCR)"]

docs.title: Code Examples
Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • Prescaler and ARR are reload-on-update. Write PSC/ARR before enabling the timer or force an update event (TIM->EGR = TIM_EGR_UG)
  • Counter frequency = (APB_CLK / (PSC+1)). Period = counter frequency / (ARR+1)

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
Timer output frequency formula?

f_out = f_apb / ((PSC+1) * (ARR+1))

What is input capture mode used for?

Measuring pulse width, frequency, or period of an external signal. The counter value is captured into CCR when an edge is detected on the input pin.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Timer: prescaler divides APB clock, counter counts to ARR, overflow triggers interrupt/PWM. f_out = f_apb / ((PSC+1)*(ARR+1)).

docs.title: UART — Async Serial Communication
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • Configure UART at a given baud rate
  • Send and receive bytes in polling and interrupt modes
  • Implement a ring buffer for interrupt-driven UART

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text:
UART is the printf of embedded systems — your first debug channel. It runs without a shared clock; both ends agree on baud rate ahead of time.

UART in one sentence

Fixed baud rate, 1 start bit + 8 data bits + 1 stop bit. No clock signal — both sides count bit periods.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant TX as TX Line
    participant Frame as UART Frame
    TX->>Frame: Start bit (low)
    Frame->>Frame: D0 D1 D2 D3 D4 D5 D6 D7
    Frame->>Frame: Parity bit (optional)
    Frame->>TX: Stop bit (high)
    note over Frame: Total 10 bits at 115200 baud = 86µs per byte

docs.title: Code Examples
Pitfalls
docs.text:
Common Mistakes

  • Always enable UART peripheral clock AND GPIO clock before configuration
  • Baud rate register value = PCLK / baud. Wrong PCLK value gives garbled output

docs.title: Flashcards
docs.text:
What baud rate is most commonly used for debug UART?

115200 baud — it's fast enough for debug output and reliable at typical MCU clock speeds.

Why use a ring buffer for UART RX?

Polling is too slow — you'll miss bytes at 115200 baud while the CPU is doing other work. The ISR stores bytes in the ring buffer; the application reads at its own pace.

What happens if RXNE is not cleared before the next byte arrives?

Overrun error (ORE flag set). The incoming byte is lost. Clear ORE by reading SR then DR.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: UART: baud = PCLK / BRR. TX: wait TXE, write DR. RX: wait RXNE, read DR. Interrupt RX: ISR writes ring buffer, app reads from it.
